text: ARCAIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sèvres, cant. of Frontenay. Pop. 918.
ARCAMBUL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, arrond. of Cahors. Pop. 1,120.

ARCANGELO (San), a small town of the Ecclesiastical states, in the deleg. of Forli, 6 m. W of Rimini. Clement was born here in 1705.— Also a town of Naples, in the Basilicata, 30 m. ENE of Lagonegro. Pop. 3,800.
ARCANGUES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenees, cant. of Bayonne. Pop. 964.

ARCAS, a small island near the mouth of the Rio Grande, in Senegambia, in N lat. 11°8', W long. 14°4'. -Also a group of small islands and rocks in the gulf of Mexico, in N lat. 20° 12′, W long. 92° 24′.- Also a river of Brazil, falling into the Maranon, near its mouth, 54 m. NE of Gurupu.
ARCASSON. See ARCACHON.
ARCE, a town in Naples, in the Terra-di-Lavora, district of Sora. Pop. 4,340.
ARCENAUT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, 6 m. N of Beaune. Pop. 404.
ARCENS, a commune of France, dep. of Ardeche, cant. of Saint-Martin-du-Valamas. Pop. 1,276.
ARCES, a commune and town of France, dep. of ARC Charente-Inférieure, 18 m. SW of Saintes. Pop. 1,120.—Also a com. in the dep. of L'Yonne, cant. of Cérisiers. Pop. 1,020.
ARCEY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. of L'Isle. Pop. 832.
ARCH, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, in a beautiful valley of the same name, 15 m. N of Berne.
ARCHAIG, a lake in Inverness-shire, 16 m. long, and 15 m. broad. It approaches to within 2 m. of the SW extremity of Loch Lochy.

ARCHANGEL, or ARCHANGELSKOE [i. e., 'the Land of the Archangel'], a prov. of Russia, on the shores of the Arctic ocean; bounded by the gov. of Tobolsk on the NE and E; by that of Wologda on the S and SE; by Olonetz on the SW; and by Finland on the NW. Its northern continental shores are washed by the Arctic ocean and the White sea; and for a considerable distance from the coast present a desolate and sterile appearance, with few signs of vegetation. It embraces the whole northern part of Russia in Europe, and includes the island of Nova Zembla. Between the Arctic ocean and its frontier conterminous with Wologda, it stretches over 8° from N to S. Its E limit is in 66°, and its W in 30° E long. Its area is estimated by Möller at 15,215 German, or 342,337 sq. m.; and by M. Kæppen at 750,906 sq. versts, or 15,519 German sq. m. As the latter calculation was carefully made upon Schubert's special map, it is probably a pretty close approximation to the fact; but in both admeasurements the superficies of the islands is included, which probably amounts to one-fourth of the whole. The surface is in general a continuous flat, covered either with sandy and mossy wastes, or pine and alder forests. The principal rivers are the Dwina and its affluents; and the Petshora and Onega. There are many lakes, amongst which are the Imandra, the Taposero, the Angosero, and the Koutno. The rivers are frozen from October to May. The severity of the climate is so great that the corn raised is unequal to the consumption of the inhabitants. The only vegetables which are cultivated with any success are hemp and flax, of both of which great quantities are grown. The pastures are good; but neither horses, cattle, nor sheep are numerous. The crown-forests in 1831 occupied about a 25th part of the surface. The wild animals are bears, wolves, foxes, ermines, and rein-deer, together with the birds common to such latitudes. The pop. in 1829 was estimated at 263,100, amongst whom were 5,000 families of Samoiedes dwelling between the White sea and the Ural mountains, and 2,000 families of Laponians between the W coasts of the White sea and the Arctic ocean. The pop. in 1846, calculated upon the principle of adding 10 per cent. to that of 1838, was 253,000, so that it does not amount to one per sq. m. The inhabitants are chiefly employed in agriculture, timber-felling, charcoal, potash, and turpentine making, in the whale and herring-fisheries, the manufacture of coarse cloth and cordage, and the preparation of skins and leather. The exports are skins, salted meats, teeth of the sea-horse, fishoil, dried cod, herrings, oxen, calves, timber, ships built at Archangel, salt, iron, pitch, and turpentine. The imports are corn, brandy, woollen and other manufactured stuffs, colonial produce, wines, and metals.—This province is divided into eight circles: viz., the city and dependencies of Archangel, Kholmogory, Shenkursk, Pinéga, Onéga, Kem, and Mezen. In 1833, there were 9 cities or towns, and 5 market towns within the prov. The chief insular dependencies are Solowezkoi, Waigatz, and Nova Zembla: which see.

ARCHANGEL, the cap. of the above, is situated on the White sea, about 35 m. from the mouth of the ! Dwina, and 400 m. NE of St. Petersburg, in N lat. 64° 32′, E long. 40° 33′. The city extends about 3 m. in length, and 1 in breadth. The houses are generally of wood, but well-contrived, and most of them are two stories high; but there is an immense pile of stone-built warehouses. For many years A. was the only seaport of Russia, but when St. Petersburg became a place of export, and Riga a Russian port, its trade diminished; till in 1762 it received the same privileges and rights as St. Petersburg. It is now frequented by traders of all nations, and is the chief entrepot of foreign trade with Siberia, being connected by canals with Moscow and Astrachan; and a road is now being constructed along the borders of the White sea, through Keni to Tornea, whence the communication with Norway is easy. The inhabitants of the different settlements on the White and Arctic seas, and near the mouths of the rivers falling into these seas, are very generally engaged in a coasting-trade, and in the fisheries on the coast of Swedish Finmark. About 300 vessels resort to these fisheries, and return before winter to port. These vessels are rudely constructed and rigged, yet they are seldom lost or wrecked. There is an imperial dockyard at A., and two or three men-of-war are generally on the stocks here. About 400 vessels enter this port annually; they arrive in June or July, and leave it in September or October. During the time they remain, a great market is held, at which | 8 m. S of Lanciano. Pop. 2,400. oil, tallow, tar, linseed, timber, hides, furs, wax, iron, and coarse linen are sold. In 1838, 334 vessels arrived, of a total tonnage of 69,742 tons, with cargoes valued at £34,686; and 333 departed with cargoes valued at £481,071. Of the imports in that year £16,114 was in British vessels; and the exports in Russian vessels amounted to £353,558. In 1842, 212 vessels arrived, of a total tonnage of 41,721 tons, with cargoes valued at £18,384; of these 153 were British, but their cargoes were valued at only £801, 150 being in ballast, and the only imports being fruit, oil, wine, books, and coals. The exports in 1842 amounted to £427,789; of which £305,823 was in British vessels. The harbour is good; but the entrance is greatly obstructed by sand-banks, affording only 12½ ft. of water. Large ships have to load and unload without the bar. it is protected by the lines of Nowadwinka which command the entrance of the river. On the neighbouring island of Solombalsk, formed by the Kushenida, about 1 m. below the town, are the admiralty and marine barracks, in which a body of from 5,000 to 6,000 seamen is generally stationed. A civil and military governor resides here; and it is the see of a prelate, who takes the title of archbishop of Archangel and Kholmogory. Besides eleven Russian churches, there is one for Lutherans, and one for Calvinists, a convent, a lyceum, a gymnasium, and a naval academy. Pop. in 1833, 18,827; houses, 2,154.—A. was made the cap, of the gov. which bears its name in 1710. In 1553, an English ship commanded by Chancellor was driven by stress of weather into the White sea, and the captain landed at the convent or abbey of St. Nicholas, and thence travelled on sledges to Moscow, where he obtained permission for eight English merchants to open a trade with Russia by way of the White sea. This was the commencement of the trade of this port, and as far back as 1655 its exports amounted to 600,000 rubles; and the annual average exports to England alone between 1691 and 1700 amounted to £112,210; and the imports to £58,881.

ARCHANGEL (New), the principal establishment of the Russian Fur company, in Russian America. It is situated in the midst of marshes, forests, and barren mountains, on an island separated from ARC the main by Norfolk sound, in N lat. 57° 3', W long. 135° 18', and is garrisoned by about 300 men. It was formerly called Sitka. In 1833 the pop. was 847, of whom one-half were Europeans, 307 creoles, or children of Russian fathers by native mothers, and 134 Aleutes and Koliushes. The mean temperature for the year at New A., 7° 39' C., indicates a mild climate; but the mean temp. at Bergen in Norway, 3° further N, is 8° 18' C.
ARCHANGELSK, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Nicolaef, on the l. bank of the Aksina, in N lat. 48° 30'. Extensive copper-works exist here, the annual produce of which is 2,500 cwt.
ARCHANGELSKOI, the name of two towns in Russia; one 90 m. NE of Vologda, the other 124 m. NNE of Kostroma.
ARCHED ISLAND, one of the Philippines, in N lat. 9° 15′, E long. 117° 57′.
ARCHENA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 14 m. NW of Murcia, on the Segura river. There are warm springs here of a temp. of 40° R., and some remains of Roman baths.

ARCHES, a commune and village in France, in the dep. of Vosges, cant. of Epinal, 36 m. SSE of Nancy, on the l. bank of the Moselle. Pop. 1,335. It is celebrated for its excellent paper manufactory.—On the opposite side of the river is another village also celebrated for its paper-mills, called Archettes. Pop. 677.
ARCHI, a town of Naples, in the Lower Abruzzo,
ARCHIAC, a canton, commune, and town of France, dep. of Charente-Inférieure, 24 m. SE of Saintes. Pop. of cant., comprising 17 com., 11,510; of com., 1,704.

ARCHIDONA, town in New Granada, 7 m. NE of Antequera. Pop. 5,000.—Also a town of Ecuador, 138 m. SE of Quito, in S lat. 0° 45', on an affluent of the Napo. Pop. 2,000. This town suffered greatly by an eruption of Cotopaxi in 1744.—Also a town of Spain, in the prov. and 19 m. NW of Malaga. Pop. 6,000. Fine marbles are quarried in the vicinity.
ARCHIGNAC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Solignac. Pop. 915.
ARCHINGEAY, a commune and town of France, dep. of Charente-Inférieure, 10 m. W of St.-Jeand'Angely. Pop. 1,085. There are sulphurous mineral springs here.

ARCHIPELAGO, a name originally given to that part of the Mediterranean, sometimes called the Ægean sea, which lies between the coasts of Greece and Asia Minor, and is remarkable for the number of islands scattered through its whole extent; but which has come to be used as a generic term, and is indifferently applied to any tract of sea which is thickly strewn with islands, or to the clusters of islands themselves. Thus we have the archipelago of Chili, the archipelago of the Philippines, the Aleutian archipelago, the Eastern or Indian archipelago, the Chagos archipelago, the Dangerous archipelago, and many other archipelagos, and archipelagic groups, which, with the exception of the Archipelago par excellence, we shall notice under their special appellations. The etymology of the term is doubtful as far as regards its first member. It may be a corruption of Ægœum; or it may be from agxos, i.e. 'chief.'

ARCHIPE LAGO (THE), or ÆGÆAN SEA, a great arm of the Mediterranean, extending from the 35th to the 41st parallel of N lat. It is enclosed on the N by the Turkish prov. of Romelia; on the E by Asia Minor or Anatolia; on the S by an imaginary line drawn from Cape Alepu, in N lat. 36° 30', E long. 27° 59', on the E, along the eastern and southern shores of the islands of Rhodes and Scarpanto, the southern shore of Caxo, the southern coast of Candia, and the western shores of the islands of Cerigotto and Cerigo, to Cape Malea or San Angelo, at the SE extremity of the Morea, in N lat. 36° 26', E long. 23° 15'; and on the W by Greece, and the Turkish province of Thessaly. It is 450 m. in length from N to S; and from 100 to 300 in breadth. Augustus Petermann F.R.G.S. tinction from the Kara-degniz or 'Black sea.' The Romans called the northern part Mare Macedonicum; the southern, Mare Gracum. By the Greeks, the sea to the N of Crete was called Mare Creticum; between Crete and Rhodes, Carpathium pelagus; near the island of Icaria, Icarium mare; between Attica and the Cyclades, Myrtoum mare; all the rest of the archipelago was called by the general names of the Mare Ægæum. This sea,—the shores of which generally consist of confused masses of calcareous rocks, and are in many places steep and rugged, -is physically remarkable for the irregularity of its outline, the number and magnitude of the promontories and gulfs along its sinuous coasts, and the number of islands, varying in dimensions from a circumference of 400 m. to mere rocky islets, with which, throughout its entire extent, it is studded. These islands are generally divided into two groups, distinguished either by the names of the continents to which they geographically belong; or by the ancient appellations of the Sporades and the Cyclades, - the Sporades denoting those scattered along the Asiatic coasts,—the Cyclades consisting of the central cluster, to the E of the Morea, and the islands along the shores of Europe.—The rivers which discharge themselves into this sea generally take their rise in mountains adjacent to its shores, and are consequently of inconsiderable dimensions. The principal are the Selembria, which flows into the gulf of Salonika on the W; the Inje-Carasu, and the Vardar towards the N; the Struma or Strymon, which enters at the gulf of Contessa; the Mesto or Carasu, flowing into the gulf of Kavalla; the Maritza, to the NW of the gulf of Enos; and, on the Anatolian shore, the Kodus and Meander, the former flowing into the gulf of Smyrna, the latter having its embouchure op- Posite the island of Guidharonisi. - Following the Outline of the coast from Cape Malea, the first in the enumeration of the principal features which it presents, is the gulf of Napoli-the Argolicus sinus-with the little island-group of the Spetziai and Makronisi, near its entrance. To the E of this gulf, between Capes Bear and Skyllo, is the rocky island of Hydra. Cape Skyllo, in N lat. 37° 26½, E long. 23° 32½, 4½ m. NNW from the E end of Hydra, forms the SW extremity of the next large gulf, that of Ægina—the Saronicus sinus—in the centre of which is the island of the same name, the ancient Enone or Myrmidonia; towards the NE the island of Koluri, the ancient Salamis; and 5 m. to the N of Cape Skyllo the sterile island of Poros. Rounding Cape Colonna—the ancient Sunium—in N lat. 37° 39′ 12″, E long. 24° 1′ 34", the channel of Negropont—the ancient Euripus -bends along the coast of Livadia, and, uniting with the channels of Talanti and Trikeri, separates the great island of Egripo or Negropont—the ancient Eubera—from the Grecian shore. This channel is remarkable for the irregularity of its tides. Sometimes the tide will run 8 m. an hour, and suddenly change to a velocity of 4 or 5 m. in the opposite direction,—a phenomenon of which no satisfactory solution has hitherto been offered. The southern entrance of this channel, between Cape Kavalla on the W, and the Petali isles on the E, is 3 leagues in breadth; 5 leagues N of this, it is contracted to a breadth of 2 m.; in its narrowest part it is crossed by a bridge only 50 fathoms in length, which connects the island of Negropont with the main. Between the N coast of Negropont, and the Thessalian coast, is the channel of Trikeri, which, towards the N, expands into the gulf of Volo. About 20 m. to the E of Negropont, is the steep and sterile island of Skyro; and nearer, extending ENE and NE from the channel of Trikeri across the mouth of the gulf of Salonika, is an island-chain known as the Thessalian isles, con- I. sisting of the small island of Skiathos, which is separated from the coast of Thessaly by the strait of St. George, the fertile island of Scopelo, Kilidromi, and several minor islands. To the NW of the Thessalian isles, the large gulf of Salonika - the Thermaicus sinus—extends 30 leagues to the NW, along the shores of the modern Greek prov. of Tricala. Indenting the peninsula of Salonika, are the gulf of Cassandra—the ancient Toronaicus sinus—and of Monte Santo; and separated from the latter by the narrow peninsula terminating in the lofty summit of Mount Athos, 6,776 ft. in height, is the gulf of Contessa—the Strymonicus sinus—whose N extremity, Cape Orphani, is 20 m. to the W of the fertile island of Thaso-the ancient Thasos-the most northerly in the Archipelago. Kavalla, on the Macedonian coast, opposite the NW coast of Thaso, is in N lat. 40° 55' 50"; but the extreme N point of the archipelago is the head of the gulf of Lagos, in N lat. 41°, W long. 25° 10'. A shallow lagune, called the gulf of Enos, indents the main near the mouth of the Maritza; and to the S of this gulf, the deep gulf of Saros runs parallel to the Dardanelles. Opposite the entrance of this latter gulf—the Melas sinus—is the island of Samothrace or Samothraki, the highest island in the Archipelago, with the exception of Candia; and 12 m. further to the SE is the island of Imbro. The three last enumerated islands form the Thracian group. To the SW of Imbro, in the middle of the Archipelago, is the fertile volcanic island of Lemnos or Stalimene, of mythological celebrity; 6 leagues SW 1 W from which lies the little island of Agio Strati. At the distance of about 10 m. from the SE extremity of Imbro, is Cape Helles or Cape Greco, on the main, in N lat. 40° 3½', E long. 26° 10' 45", which, with Cape Yenisher or Janisari, on the coast of Anatolia, in N lat. 39° 59½, E long. 26° 11'—the ancient Sigaum — forms the entrance of the straits of the Dardanelles, 13 leagues in length, through which the current from the sea of Marmora sets southward into the A. at the rate of from 2 to 4 m. an hour. Cape Yenisher bears NE by N 3 leagues from the NE point of the once flourishing island of Tenedos, called by the Turks, as the key of the Hellespont, Bogtcha- Adassi; and between Cape Baba—the ancient Lectum -on the main, and Cape Sigri on Mitylene, a channel 5 m. in breadth leads into the gulf of Adramytthe Adramyttenum sinus-which, with the channel, by which it communicates with the gulf of Smyrna, separates the great island of Mitylene, famous under the name of Lesbos in classical story, from the coast of Anatolia. This island lies between the parallels of 38° 58' and 39° 24'; and the channel by which it is separated from the main is from 7 to 10 m. in breadth. The gulf of Sandarli indents the coast of the main to the SE of Mitylene; and between Red cape, and Karaburun or Black cape, is the entrance to the deep gulf of Smyrna. The strait of Scio or Bianca, 7 leagues in length, separates the fertile island of Scio -the ancient Chios-from the coast of Anatolia, and leads into the gulf of Skala-nova, enclosed on the S by the mountainous island of Samos. The mouth of this gulf lies between Cape Koraka on the N-the ancient Corycus -- in N lat. 38° 6', and Cape Samos on the S, two headlands nearly on the same meridian, and 23 m. distant from each other. Nicaria—the ancient Icaros—is separated from Samos on the E by a channel called the Great Boghaz, 10 m. in breadth. About 6 leagues to the SE of this island, in the Icarium mare of the ancients, is the little rocky island of Patino or Patmos, remarkable as the scene of the exile and revelations of St. John; and stretching in the same direction to the SE of Patmos, are the small islands of Lero and Calymno, at the mouth of the gulf of Hassan-Kalasi, the ancient Mendalia. Between the gulfs of Hassan-Kalasi and Kos, is the peninsula of Budrum. The island of Kos, now commonly called, in corrupt Romaic, Stanchio, has its NE point nearly in N lat. 36° 54', E long. 27° 16', and stretches 23 m. to the SW, forming, with the long peninsular strip of land terminating in Cape Krio on the main, the entrance to the gulf of Kos. To the S of Cape Krio, in N lat. 36° 40′ 46", E long. 27° 21′ 6", is the gulf of Syme, which encloses the high rocky barren island of the same name, and several minor islets, and terminates at Cape Alepu. Of the Cyclades, or central cluster of islands, Andros, the most northerly, is separated from Negropont by the strait of Silota, the scene of the catastrophe of the Grecian fleet on its return from Troy. To the SE are the islands of Tino or Tenos, and Myconi or Myconus, the former next to Scio in commercial prosperity. Between Myconus and the cold and mountainous but productive island of Syra or Syros, lie the two celebrated islands, the Greater and the Lesser Delos, called by the modern Greeks, the Dili, and by Turkish mariners, Isdili. SSE of the latter island is the volcanic and mountainous island of Naxia or Naxos, the largest of the Cyclades, being 17 m. from N to S, and about 12 in its greatest breadth. Its N point is in N lat. 37° 12'. W of Naxia is Paros, famous for its quarries of white marble; and about 2 m. further to the W, is the celebrated island of Antiparos. SE of Naxos lies the mountainous island of Amorgo or Amorgos; and further in the same direction, is the island of Stampalia or Astypalea, called from its luxuriance Theon-trapeza, i. e. 'the Table of the gods.' To the S of Amorgo is the small island of Nauphio or Anaphi; and 4 leagues further to the W, forming the most southernly of the Cyclades, is the crescent-shaped island of Santorin, the ancient Kallisto or Thera, lying between lat. 36° 20' and 36° 28' S. This and the smaller surrounding islands abound in traces of volcanic origin. To the NNW is the productive island of Nio, the ancient Ios; 4 m. to the W is the fertile and mountainous island of Sikyno; 5 m. to the W of which lies Polycandro—the ancient Pholegandros—remarkable only for its ruggedness and sterility. The insalubrious and now almost depopulated island of Milo or Melos, is distinguished by containing Mount St. Elias, the highest in the Archipelago, next to Athos. Its summit is in N lat. 36° 40′ 27″, E long. 24° 23′ 23″. On the NE side of this island, and separated by a channel not above a mile in breadth, is the lofty volcanic Argentura, the ancient Cimolis or Cimolas; and 5 m. to the NE of this island is the pretty and productive Siphnos or Siphanto, the ancient Merapia or Acis. About 8 m. NNW lies the circular-shaped Serpho, the ancient Scriphus. Northwards is the fertile island of Thermia or Cythnos; and still further N, inclining towards the channel of Negropont, is the once famous and still important island of Zea. A large proportion of these islands are of volcanic origin; some are granitic, while others are calcareous, consisting often of marble of the finest description. They are generally of considerable elevation; and the larger contain well-watered plains and vallies of great fertility and beauty. Their principal productions are wheat, fruits of various kinds, particularly the vine, olives, figs, lemons and oranges, cotton in small quantities of the finest description, silk, honey, wax, and gum-mastick. The woods which formerly covered the summits are now to a great extent destroyed. Besides marble, some of the larger islands contain sulphur, iron, and alum. Sponge is obtained in considerable quantities on the coasts of the Sporades. Sheep are reared in considerable numbers. Hares are abundant; and the shores abound with perch, urchin and cuttle fish. The manufactures of the islands ARC consist principally in wine, oil, and dried fruits; and their commerce is limited to the interchange of articles of local produce and local consumption, carried on by means of kaïks, or open canoe-shaped boats, with one large sprit-sail. Their towns-which generally present a sad contrast to the once large and stately cities with the ruins of which they are still thickly bestrewn—are commonly built in an amphitheatrical form, on the brow of a rising ground near the shore. The islands are generally scantily peopled. The islanders are a fine athletic race, expert in every species of maritime craft. Their principal occupation is fishing. Their dress consists of a jacket without a collar, a waistcoat, full breeches reaching only to the knees, and fastened with a red sash round the waist, a small red cowl, and slipper-shaped shoes. They wear the hair thrown to the back of the head, and allow it to grow to a great length, and generally use mustachios. The women-especially those of the Cyclades-are remarkable for their beauty. Their principal article of dress is a long jacket, generally trimmed with fur. Their usual head-dress is a red cap. Their chief occupations, in addition to those of the household, which are generally of a laborious description, are knitting, spinning, and weaving. The prevailing winds in the A. are the NE or Etesian, which blow for several months in autumn. They are succeeded by variable weather and winds, and by the rainy season; after which follow N and NNE winds, not unfrequently accompanied with sleet and snow. Rain chiefly falls during ESE and SSE winds. From April to October, the weather is generally unbroken. The current in this sea sets almost continually to the southward. The Ægæan sea, stretching along the coasts of Ionia, the Troad, Mysia, Lydia, Caria, and Lycia, in Asia, and those of Thrace, Macedonia, Thessaly, Beotia, Eubœa, Attica, and the Peloponnesus, in Europe, was the scene of many memorable and eventful transactions in the early history of Greece and of Asia Minor. The islands of the A. were early divided betwixt the Persians and the Greeks. On the fall of the Eastern empire, the Venetians seized upon several of them; and in the 13th cent., Marco Sanuto, a Venetian, having made himself master of Naxos, and several of the neighbouring islands, assumed the title of duke of the Archipelago. In the 16th cent. the Turks were masters of the entire archipelago, and united these islands to the sanjak of Gallipoli, under the special superintendence of the capitan pasha. The knights of Malta attempted their liberation from Turkish rule, and drove the Turkish part of the inhabitants to the continent; but the Porte retained sufficient authority over the islands to exact a tribute or capitation tax from them until the breaking out of the Greek revolution in 1821. The Cyclades now form a separate nomos or department of the kingdom of Greece. The surveys of Captain Gualtier of the French marine, and Captains Smyth and Beaufort in the English service, have mainly contributed to our accurate knowledge of this island-sprinkled sea.
ARCHITAS, a town of Albania, 19 m. N of Joannina, on an affluent of the Vujutzos.
ARCHLEBAU, a town of Moravia, in the circle of Brunn, 2 m. W of Steinitz. Pop. 893. Excellent wine is made here.
ARCHODILLA, a village at the S extremity of the island of Corfu, near Cape Bianco.
ARCHSHOFEN, a village and bailiwick of Wurtemberg, on the Tauber, 3 m. E of Creglingen. Pop. 430.
ARCHUDA, a town of Albania, in the district of Toli-Monastir.
ARCHUDI, a small Ionian island to the SE of Santa Maura. It is half-a-league long from N to S, and affords pasturage for goats and sheep. Its S end | is in N lat. 38° 32', E long. 20° 43'.
ARCIDOSSO, a town of Tuscany, in the prov. of Sienna, 30 m. NE of Grosseto. Pop. 2,815. It lies between Monte Amiata and Monte Labro.
ARCIER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. and 5 m. NE of Besancon.
ARCINS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Castelnau-de-Medoc. Pop. 306. The celebrated Haut-Medoc wine is made here.
ARCISA, a small town in the duchy of Milan, 10 m. W of Como.

ARCIS-SUR-AUBE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aube. The arrond, has an area of 138,000 hectares, and comprises the four cantons of Arcis-sur-Aube, Chavanges, Méry-sur-Seine, and Ramerupt. Pop. in 1836, 35,744; in 1846, 36,443. The cant., which comprises 21 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 10,193. The com. and town had a pop. in 1846 of 2,792. The town contains cotton and spinning manufactories, manufactories of cotton hosiery, and some tanneries and breweries; and carries on a considerable trade in wine, timber, charcoal, corn, flour, iron, and iron-wire.
ARCISATE, a town of Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, 10 m. W by N of Como.
ARCISZEWO, a village of Poland, in the obwody of Plock. Pop. 180.

ARCO, a strong town and castle of Tyrol, in the circle of Inspruck, on the Sarca, near the head of Lago di Garda, 12 m. W of Trent. Pop. 2,050. Quarries of marble exist here.—Also a river of South America, a branch of the Guarapiche.—Also a town of Sicily, in the Val-di-Noto.—Also a small island to the N of Madagascar.
ARCOLA, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. of Levante, 3 m. SE of Vezzano. Pop. 1,000.

ARCOLE, a village of Lombardy, 15 m. E by S of Verona, on the Alpone. Pop. 900. It is strongly situated, in a military point of view, in the midst of marshes and canals. Here, in a three days' conflict, on the 15th, 16th, and 17th of Nov., 1796, the Austrians were defeated by Bonaparte, and compelled to raise the siege of Mantua.—Also an island on the N coast of New Holland, in S lat. 15°, E long. 124° 35'.
ARCOLO, a village of Canara, in Hindostan, formerly a large town, but taken and destroyed by General Matthews, after the fall of Tippoo Saib.

ARCONA, a town of Prussia, on the peninsula of Wittov, in the island of Rugen. Here stood an old Vandal castle, and the chief temple of the god Sivantewite, whose worship was put down by Waldemar I. of Denmark, in 1168. Cape Arcona, in N lat. 54° 41' 4", E long. 13° 27' 53", forms the most northern Point of Germany, if Prussia Proper be not included therein. A lighthouse, with a fixed light, at an elevation of 188 ft. above sea-level, is established on this elevated promontory.
ARCONATE, a village of Lombardy, 19 m. WNW of Milan. Pop. 650.
ARCONCIEL, or ERGENZACH, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Friburg, 6 m. SSW of Friburg, on the Sarine. Pop. 315.
ARCONSAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Saint-Remy. Pop. 1,937.
ARCORE, a town of Lombardy, in the prov. of Milan, 13 m. NE of Milan. Pop. 2,000.

ARCOS, or ARCOS-DE-LA-FRONTERA-so called from its frontier position—a fortified town of Spain, in Andalusia, situated on a craggy rock almost insulated by the Guadalette, 30 m. ENE of Cadiz. It is surrounded by a hilly and fertile country, and celebrated for its breed of fine horses. Pop. 10,000.—Also a small town in Old Castile, on the Xalon, 9 m. from Medina-Celi.—Also a town of Portugal, in the dis- ARC trict of Lamego, in Beira, 12 m. SSE of Pesquiera. Pop. 1,800.—Also an island in the gulf of Mexico, in N lat. 20° 15′, W long. 92°.
ARCOS (Los), a town in Navarre, bearing the title of county, 13 m. SW of Estella.
ARCOS DE VALDEVEZ, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Entre Douro-e-Minho, 21 m. E of Viana. It belongs to the house of Noronha, bears the title of county, and has some trade. Pop. 2,000.

ARCOT, an extensive maritime district of Hindostan, on the Carnatic, formerly an independent state, but since 1802, subject to Britain, and under the government of the Madras presidency. It is divided into two districts or sub-collectorates,-Northern A., and Southern A., which are separated from each other by the Palaur river.—Northern A. is bounded on the N by Cuddapah and Nellore; on the E by the ocean and the Chingleput district; on the S by the Palaur; and on the W by the balaghaut of Cuddapah. It comprises an area of 8,200 sq. m., with a pop. of 506,831. Its revenue in 1817 was 734,325 pagodas; in 1835-6, £227,718. Its chief towns are Arcot, Vellore, Wallajanaghur, and Tripety.—Southern A. is bounded on the N by the Palaur; on the E by the ocean and the Chingleput district; on the S by Tangore and Trichinopoly; and on the W by Salem and the Carnatic balaghaut. Its area is 4,500 sq. m., with a pop. in 1836 of 550,239. In 1817 its revenue. amounted to 647,954 pagodas; in 1835-6, to £240,967. Its chief ports are Cuddalore, Pondicherry, and Portonovo.—The country is low throughout the maritime districts; but rises into hills further inland, and presents large tracts of jungle. Agriculture is the principal employment of the pop., who are chiefly Mahommedans. Cotton-stuffs are manufactured at Pulicat, and there is an iron-foundry at Portonovo.

ARCOT, the cap. of the above district and of the Carnatic, is situated on the S side of the river Palaur —which is here half-a-mile wide, but contains little water in the dry season—68 m. WSW of Madras, in N lat. 12° 54', E long. 79° 23'. The town is extensive, and surrounds a large fort. It is supposed to be the Soramundalum of Ptolemy, from which Coromandel is derived; but the present town is of modern date. The Mogul armies, after they had captured Ginji, were forced to remove, in consequence of the unhealthy situation, to the plains of A.; this circumstance led to the establishment of the city of A. in 1716. Anwarud Deen, nabob of Arcot, having been killed in battle in 1749, the town was taken by Chundashaheb, supported by the French. In 1751 it was retaken by Clive. It was afterwards taken by the French, but was recaptured in 1760 by Coote, after the battle of Wandiwash. In 1780 it was besieged by Hyder Ali, who gained possession of it on the 30th September, after having defeated the British under Colonel Baillie.
ARCS (LES), a commune and town of France, dep. of Var, on the Argens, 39 m. NE of Toulon. Pop. 2,448. There is an iron mine in the neighbourhood, and several oil-mills.

ARCTIC CIRCLE, one of the smaller circles of the sphere, parallel with the equator, and 23° 28' distant from the North pole. It passes through Cape North in Iceland, the Maelstrom whirlpool, the mouth of the Obi river, Behring's straits, and the S of Melville island. Supposing the surface of the globe divided into a hundred parts, about 4.1 of these will be included within each of the Arctic and Antarctic circles.

ARCTIC HIGHLANDS, a country on the NE coast of Baffin's bay, discovered by Captain Ross in 1818. It lies between N lat. 76° and 77° 40', and W long. 60° and 72°, extending 120 m. in a NW direction, and bounded on the S by an immense mountainbarrier covered with ice, with cliffs 1,000 ft. in height, and spurs of solid ice projecting for miles into the sea. The vegetable productions are heath, moss, and coarse grass which affords shelter to hares and game. The natives are Esquimaux of a dirty copper colour, and about 5 ft. in stature. It is inhabited by a tribe of Esquimaux, who, until visited by Captain Ross's exploring party, supposed themselves to be the only people in the world! ARCTIC OCEAN AND REGIONS. Under this designation is of course included all that portion of the globe which lies between the Arctic circle and the N pole, comprising large districts of land in the form of sea-coasts, promontories, peninsulas, and islands, as well as the vast expanse of the surrounding ocean. Thus, beginning from Iceland, whose N coast nearly coincides with the Arctic circle, and proceeding eastwards, we shall pass over the N extremity of Norway and Lapland, the deeply indented coast of Siberia, the N coast of N America, and a large portion of Greenland. The islands of Nova Zembla, Spitzbergen, Cockburn isle, Melville isle, and many others less perfectly known lie within the Arctic limits. But the term Arctic regions is frequently applied with a wider signification to the northern districts of Europe, Asia, and America. The principal gulfs and bays of the Arctic sea are the entrance to the White sea, the gulf of Kara, Obi, and Yenisei, Coronation gulf, the gulf of Boothia, and Baffin's bay. The principal rivers flowing into this ocean are the Obi 2,320 m. in length, the Yenisei 2,800 m., the Lena 2,400 m., and several others of less extent in Siberia; the Mackenzie 2,120 m., and the Saskatchevan 1,664 m., in America. It communicates with the N Pacific ocean by Behring's straits; and the recent surveys of Captains Franklin, Ross, Parry and Back, and Messrs. Dease and Simpson, have shown that it communicates with the Atlantic on the E coast of America, proving that the two continents of N and S America constitute one vast island, while the three combined continents of the Old world constitute another. Productions.] The bleak and inhospitable regions of the frozen zone teem with animal life: the whale, the walrus, and the seal, not only afford food, light, and clothing to the inhabitants, but invite the adventurous of more favoured climes; and countless multitudes of herrings annually migrate to the coasts of Europe. The bear, the wolf, and the fox, are the antagonists, the dog and the rein deer are the friends and coadjutors, the musk ox and the elk, or moose deer, and hares of various kinds, supply the nourishment of the inhabitants. Innumerable flocks of birds, the auk, the petrel and the gull, the swan, the goose and the duck, the tern, the plover and the ptarmigan, animate the air or the waters; and the spring and summer are cheered with the notes of many song birds [Dr. Richardson], while the sea is tinged of an olive green colour by the presence of medusæ of various sizes, microscopic and otherwise, in numbers which the imagination fails to approximate. Insect species are few; but in the short and sudden summer even mosquitoes are found. The vegetable world is likewise very limited. The American spruce or white pine, is said by Dr. Richardson to be "the only tree that the Esquimaux of the Arctic sea have access to while growing." They are chiefly supplied by drift-wood. The mosses and lichens are the principal products of the soil. There are also mushrooms, ferns, algæ, and confervæ. The summer is not wholly destitute of the ranunculus, the anemone, and the poppy,-the strawberry, the raspberry, and some other edible fruits. The Protococcus nivalis, a minute cryptogamic plant, imparts a general rosy tinge, which has occasioned the inconsistent appellation of red snow. Geology.] As far as the geological structure of these regions has been examined, the results have been found to correspond with those of similar researches elsewhere, "affording a proof of the uniformity of character, similarity of position, and universality of distribution of primitive rocks in all parts of the world." "Arctic geology exhibits the same series of rocks, and the same general arrangements as occur in other countries." "These regions abound in primitive and transition rocks; and although secondary rocks occupy considerable tracts, still, as far as is known at present, their extent is more limited than that of the older formations. The alluvial deposits are not extensive. True modern volcanic rocks occur only in Jan Mayen's island, situated between N lat. 70° 49' and 71° 8' 20", and W long. 7° 26' and 8° 44'. The only traces of tertiary strata are found in the sandstones and clays and limestones connected with the new trap rocks in Baffin's bay. The black or common coal has been discovered in Melville island by Parry, and in Jameson's land on the Greenland coast by Scoresby. Valuable ores of copper, lead, and tin, and graphite, have been met with, as well as precious garnets, beryls, zircons, dichroites, and rock crystals. Previously to the deposition of the coal formation, the older hills supported a vegetation resembling that which at present characterizes the tropical regions. Corals, of which the prototypes are at present met with in the seas of the tropical regions, also intimate that the waters of the Arctic ocean were so constituted as to support corals resembling those of the Equatorial seas." [Jameson.] Fossil remains, animal and vegetable, are not wanting in these regions, and many of these point to a similar conclusion. Large blocks or boulders of gneiss, granite, and quartz, abound in different quarters at considerable distances (some hundred miles) from their original position, with evident indications of the action of water; implying either extensive inundations or submarine earthquakes. Climate.] The general character of the climate in these regions is that of intense cold, ranging so low as 50° below zero, or even much lower. In a journal kept by Capt. Back at Fort Reliance, lat. 62° 50', the thermometer is recorded to have stood at 70° be- ARCTIC OCEAN. low zero. Yet in the brief summer the heat becomes oppressive and engenders clouds of mosquitoes. It has been observed that the extreme point of cold as indicated by the thermometer is often less severely felt in these regions than a more moderate temperature, the air in such circumstances being calm and clear. Icebergs.] The ice consists of two distinct kinds,—that arising from the congelation of salt water is "porous, incompact, and imperfectly diaphanous," and consists of thin flakes which detain in the interstices the stronger brine. It seldom exceeds 6 ft. in thickness, and projects only 1-50th above the surface. It requires about 27° Fahr. to congeal sea-water. Ice therefore is formed annually on the surface of the sea, which, gradually melting on the return of spring, is broken by the agitation of the waves into large fields of ice, which are carried southwards and dissolve. These are to be distinguished from the icebergs, which are the produce of melted snow accumulating in successive layers along the shores and bays, till, after the lapse of centuries, they attain a vast size, when the base being worn by the waves they are precipitated into the deep. It is estimated that the height of some floating icebergs is 2,000 ft., of which 200 are above the surface of the sea. They consist of a clear, compact, solid ice, affording fine soft water. When carried into southern latitudes they rapidly dissolve. At 42° it is calculated they would lose the thickness of an inch every hour. Large bodies of ice are seldom found further S than the parallel of 48°. Meteorology.] The meteorological phenomena of these regions are of a highly interesting character. Besides the recurrence of day and night protracted to a length very different from that which is usually indicated by those terms, and varying in proportion to the latitude, the Aurora Borealis cannot fail to strike the observer with wonder and admiration during the winter nights. These beautiful streams of light, forming or tending to form an irregular arch, with "showers of rays" shooting in every direction. brilliant and rapid as lightning, supply in some degree the absence of the sun, and impart an air of fairy-land to the scene. They are variously described,-by some they have been supposed to be attended with "a hissing and cracking noise" [Hearne]: but this has been contradicted by others. To Capt. Parry the light appeared to be tinged with yellow and blue; while to Capt, Lyon it resembled the milky way or vivid sheet-lightning. From the observations of Capt. Back, the Aurora Borealis appears to exert a sensible influence on the magnetic needle. The sun and moon are often surrounded here with halos tinted with the brightest hues. Parhelia, or mock suns, shine in different parts of the firmament, several in number, and most brilliant about the period of sunrise and sunset; and various luminous meteors, due probably to the refraction of the light by the crystals of ice, adorn at uncertain intervals the northern sky. Whale fishery.] The most important feature of the Arctic ocean, in a commercial point of view, is the whale fishery. This pursuit has been carried on by the northern nations from a very early period: but it is only within a comparatively recent date that it has engaged the attention of nations more remote from the haunts of the whale. The English having claimed the discovery of Spitzbergen by Sir H. Willoughby, claimed also the exclusive right of fishing off the coasts of that island, where whales abounded: an unwarrantable assumption—which led to frequent collision with other nations, particularly the Dutch, engaged in the same pursuits. Their eagerness to establish their right prevented their obtaining the amount of advantage which a more generous line of conduct would have afforded; and the profits of the trade were nearly counterbalanced by the efforts to secure a monopoly. The Dutch were for a long time the most successful fishers; and they founded a colony on the island where the valuable produce of the whale might be prepared before being brought home, thus saving the carriage of unprofitable cargo. This flourished most prosperously until the whales themselves migrated to some other seas— affording a remarkable instance of sagacity. These animals reposed in quiet security when their abodes were first visited by men; but since the intrusion of their only superior adversary they have changed from sea to sea to avoid his ravages. From the coasts of Spitzbergen they betook themselves to the open sea, thence to the great bank of ice forming the western boundary of the Whale-fishers bight in the Greenland sea. This broke up the Dutch colony at Sneerenberg, and the whale-fishing monopoly of Holland, but the trade was thereby thrown open. In England, during the 18th century, it was carried on so languidly, that a bounty of 20, 30, and even 40 shillings a tun was allowed to every ship exceeding 200 tons engaged in the trade, -nor was this bounty entirely withdrawn till 1824, though the increasing prosperity of the British whale fishery had long before rendered it a lucrative employment. It is one, however, which requires great outlay, and is exposed to unusual risks. The sum required for a single whaler of about 350 tons (recommended by Scoresby as the most eligible size) with her outfit, is at a moderate estimation reckoned at £8,000; and if she return clean, that is without any capture, the entire loss will exceed £2,000, while the total expense of a voyage which produces 200 tons of oil is estimated at Hull at £3,500 without insurance. Progressive geography.] Our knowledge of these regions is due principally to the efforts made at various times and by various nations to effect a more expeditious passage to India than any previously known,-an aim to which we owe almost all our important maritime discoveries, not excepting those of Columbus. Among the earliest navigators in high latitudes were John Cabot and his son Sebastian, merchants in Bristol, in the time of Henry VII., who discovered Newfoundland and Labrador. Sebastian Cabot, after having retired from actual service, became a great authority in maritime questions, and was appointed grand pilot of England by Edward VI. Under his instructions an expedition was fitted out under the command of Sir Hugh Willoughby and Richard Chancelor, directed to the discovery of a passage by the NE. Willoughby reached Nova Zembla, but in attempting to return was cast on the Russian shore, and of his ultimate fate no satisfactory account has ever been given. Chancelor entered the White sea, and from that point opened up a communication with was lost in Pitsligo bay in Scotland. Various other attempts were made in the same direction, the most successful being that under Bareutz, in the service of the United provinces, who reached the northern extremity of Nova Zembla, N lat. 77°, and advanced as far E as the gulf of Obi, which he mistook for the extremity of Asia, and therefore, instead of prosecuting his voyage, returned home. His account led to another expedition (1595) which, however, only reached the island of Waygatz. He subsequently discovered Bear or Cherry island, and Spitzbergen or East Greenland, attaining the lat. 80° N. He died on his voyage home from Nova Zembla, whither he had gone again in the prosecution of the original design of effecting the passage by NE. In 1608 some London merchants sent out Hudson with the same view. His efforts, however, were not crowned with success; but his later voyages, directed to the NW, led to the discovery of that vast bay which bears his name. In an attempt to reach the N pole he surveyed a part of the Greenland coast, and reached 81% N lat. Efforts had been early made in this direction. Cortereal, supported by Portugal, had discovered Labrador, and perhaps Hudson's straits, but on a second voyage was lost. Several expeditions, generally unfortunate in their issue, were equipped-from England in the reign of Henry VIII. In that of Queen Elizabeth, Martin Frobisher made several voyages; and a sound-not within the strict Arctic region—bears his name. In 1585, John Davis left England with the same view, for the first time. In later voyages he advanced to within the Arctic circle in those straits which retain his name. In 1610, Hudson, already experienced in northern navigation, and long bent upon attempting a NW passage, set sail with this view and entered Hudson's bay. On returning his crew mutinied, and abandoned him to a fate which has never been clearly known. In 1616, Bylot and Baffin were sent out by the Dutch with instructions to pass up Davis's straits, if possible to 80°. The highest actually attained was 78°; and the sea thus traversed was called Baffin's bay. Some passages were examined in 1631 by Capt. James and Fox. In 1741, Capt. Middleton reached Repulse bay. And little more was attempted till very recently, when, in 1818, the admiralty sent out Capt. Ross and Lieut. Parry. The former of these returned from the expedition after having explored Baffin's bay and the entrance to Lancaster sound, which he somewhat too harshly concluded was impenetrably closed. Parry, more sanguine of a passage, in a later voyage, attained through that channel the W long. 110°, and in his course discovered and named many islands in that high latitude. By the exercise of great skill and judgment he was able to spend a winter in these frozen regions without diminution of his crew, excepting the loss of one man who died of a disease with which he had been affected previous to the voyage. The attempt was repeated in a lower latitude, but owing to the loss of one of his vessels he was obliged to return, but not before he had again entered the great Arctic ocean N of America. Cotemporary with the expeditions of Cap. Parry to this ocean from the E coast of N America, attempts had been made from the land by Cap. Franklin and Dr. Richardson. These enterprising travellers, with the greatest perseverance, at the cost of very severe sufferings, were able, in two journeys, reaching the sea by following the course of the Coppermine r. and M'Kenzie r., to survey a long line of coast extending from Point Turnagain N lat. 69°, W long. 109°, to Return Reef, N lat. 70°, W long. 149°. The aim of the last expedition had been to reach Icy cape, the farthest point attained by Cap. Cook in 1776, by way of Behring's straits (discovered by the navigator whose name they bear, in the service of Catherine of Russia in 1730), and Cap. Beechey had been sent out with orders to meet him by that route. The two parties, however, not being able to unite, an extent of about 146 miles was left unexplored on the W coast, while a much larger extent, viz., the interval between the points attained by Franklin and those attained by Back and Ross, had not been explored in the W. The completion of the survey was accomplished by two officers of the Hudson's Bay Co., Messrs. Dease and Simpson, by whom each of these blanks was filled up in the years 1836-1839. Attempts have been made at different times to reach the N Pole, generally under the idea of discovering a more expeditious route to India. Among the earliest navigators engaged in this bold undertaking was Hudson, who was sent out by the Muscovy Co. in 1607. He attained a lat. of 81°, but returned with a conviction that the design was impracticable. He was followed by James Poole, who does not appear to have reached so high a lat. as Hudson; nor does Fortherby (1614) seem to have surpassed him. From this time the attempt, though not absolutely relinquished, was not revived till the reign of George III., when an expedition was fitted out under Cap. Phipps, afterwards Lord Mulgrave, but with no better success. In 1806, Mr. Scoresby made the nearest well- calcareous hill in the neighbourhood, which contains ARC authenticated approach to the Pole up to that period, namely 812°. Cotemporary with Cap. Ross's expedition in search of the NW passage, Cap. Buchan was sent out to explore the Polar seas; he is said to have reached 80° 32', but the result was as on previous occasions. The attempt to reach the N Pole by ships was therefore abandoned, and it was proposed by Mr. Scoresby to employ vehicles drawn by dogs or rein-deer. The idea was adopted by Captain Parry; but the regions which they had expected to find a level plain of ice, proved to be so beset with "hummocks," or masses of ice, as to render the rein-deer and the wheeled carriages useless, and to make the journey insuperably difficult. They were quite unable to press beyond 82° 40'. No better success attended the expedition of Cap. Ross in 1829; whose long protracted absence led to the journey of Cap. Back in search of him. The Royal Society having urged the Admiralty to make one further attempt to solve the 300 years' problem of a NW passage by sea from the Atlantic to the Pacific ocean, an expedition under Sir John Franklin sailed in 1845, with instructions to make Lancaster sound on the W side of Baffin's bay, and entering it to push westwards in the lat. of about 74% till he reached the long. of Cape Walker in about 98° W; and from this point to endeavour to penetrate to the S and W in a course as direct as could be maintained to Bhering's strait. If he should fail to accomplish this Sir John was instructed to return as far as Wellington channel, on the N side of Barrow's strait, and to endeavour to ascend it, and penetrate westwards, after getting to the N of the Parry islands. It is not known which of these alternatives the expedition may have attempted, as up to the moment of our penning these lines, [Dec. 1849] no accounts have reached this country of Sir John's vessels, the Erebus and Terror, since they were seen in Baffin's bay. Sir James Ross has returned from a fruitless expedition in search of Sir John Franklin's party, having found it impossible to reach either Cape Walker or Wellington straits, and having discovered no traces of the missing ships; while Sir John Richardson's search for them along the N coast of the American continent, between the Mackenzie and the Coppermine river, has been equally unsuccessful. A hope is yet entertained that Sir John Franklin's ships are shut up in some of the passages between Victoria, Banks', and Wollaston's lands; or may, perhaps, have reached some point of the coast between Cape Barrow and the Great Fish river. Native tribes.] The inhabitants of the coasts and islands of the Arctic ocean, though differing considerably, partake in a greater or less degree of the same character, due perhaps to their peculiar circumstances which expose them to a climate of the utmost severity, and to the somewhat capricious alternations of scarcity and abundance. Hence, among all, we find the same unequal struggle against cold and famine which leaves neither opportunity nor capability for mental cultivation, such as those enjoy who are blessed with a more genial climate. Yet even within these districts the character is modified partly by circumstances sufficiently obvious, partly by others very difficult to detect. Thus, we can readily understand how a longer continued intercourse with more civilized nations may ameliorate the character, and therefore that the Norwegians, the Laplanders, and the Siberians may excel the Esquimaux, being subjected to European governments and institutions, while it is less easy to account for the varieties which we find among the different native tribes of N. America, whose national characters differ greatly at very small local intervals. In disposition the Esquimaux are gentle and inoffensive; the early navigators were received by them with friendship and hospitality, and their intercourse was marked by the most exemplary honesty: if, as is stated, this virtue abated with greater familiarity, it may be questioned whether the deviation may not be ascribed to the evil example, and especially the cupidity of the adventurers. The general standard of height is lower among the Esquimaux than that of the European: the trunk is thick, the extremities are small; the faces broad and flat; and nose small, and with little elevation. Resemblance in their general features as well as similarity in the habits of life, and especially affinity of speech, seem to unite, under one common family, the whole of the inhabitants of the polar regions, the Esquimaux, the Greenlanders, Samoiedes, and the Kamtschadales-modified, no doubt, by the difference in political and moral relations, especially by the comparative extent to which they have been subject to the influence of Christianity.
ARCTIC SOUND, an inlet in Coronation gulf, in Arctic America, in N lat. 67° 20′, W long. 110°.

ARCUEIL, a commune and village of France, 31 m. S from Paris, in the cant. of Villejuif on the Bièvre. Pop. 1,816. It is remarkable for the aqueduct constructed by Mary of Medicis, in 1624, to convey water from Rungis to Paris. There are stone-quarries in the neighbourhood.
ARCUQUINSAS, a town of Texas, on Galveston bay.

ARCY-SUR-CURE, a commune, village, and town of France, dep. of Yonne, on the river Cure, 16 m. SSE of Auxerre. Pop. 1,500. There is a * some curious caves abounding in stalactites. They are described by Buffon.

ARD, or AR, a primitive root diffused throughout many languages, and in geographical nomenclature generally signifying 'a height.' Thus in 'Hebrew ar or har is 'a mountain.' In the Celtic, ard signifies 'a height.' In the Sanscrit, according to Tod, Arboodha is 'the hill of Boodha;' Aravalli, 'the hill of strength.' In the ancient Persian, ard, art, or artha, signifies 'great' or 'high.' Hence the frequent occurrence of this particle in geographical names.
ARD, a river of Germany, rising in Nassau, near Weher, and falling into the Lahn at Dietz.

ARD (Loch), a lake in the parish of Aberfoyle, in Perthshire, 3 m. in length by ½ in breadth. It lies at the eastern base of Ben Lomond. The Forth flows through this lake, or may be regarded as rising in it and Loch Con, a smaller lake a little to the W.

ARDA, a river of Romelia, which rises in Mount Balkan, and flowing from E to W, falls into the Maritza, near Adrianople, after a course of about 105 m. Its course is rapid.—Also a town on the l. bank of this river, in about N lat. 41° 22'.
ARDACKER, a market-town of Lower Austria, in the Upper Winerwald, on the Danube, 10 m. SW of Ips.

ARDAGH, a parish in co. Cork, 41 m. NNW of Youghal. Area 7,800 acres. Pop. 2,552. It is intersected by the Toorig, a tributary of the Blackwater.—Also a p. in co. Limerick, 5 m. W of Rathkeale. Area 9,030 acres. Pop. 2,223. The v. of A. is 4 m. N of Newcastle. Pop. 386.—Also a p. in co. Mayo, lying around the head of Lough Conn, 2 m. WSW of Ballina. Area 5,494 acres. Pop. 2,621.—Also a p. in co. Meath, 4½ m. NE of Nobber. Area 3,669 acres. Pop. 2,518.—Also a barony in co. Longford. Area 40,223 acres. Pop. 19,917.— Also a p. and v. in the barony of A. Area of p. 11,447 acres. Pop. 4,524. The v. is 2½ m. SSW of Edgeworthstown. Pop. 165.—Also a bishopric in the prov. of Armagh, which was consolidated with that of Elphin and Kilmore in 1833.
ARDAHAN, a town of Turkish Armenia, NW of Kars, on a river of the same name, one of the head-streams of the Kur. It has a citadel, and is walled round.
ARDAL, a town of Norway, in the prov. of N Bergen, 70 m. N of Christiania, in N lat. 61° 17', E long. 7° 24'. It stands on the banks of a lake which communicates with the N sea by the Sogne-fiord.
ARDALES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 31 m. W by N of Malaga, on a river of the same name.
ARDAM, a village of Greece, in Thessaly, 8 m. ENE of Trikala, at the southern base of a mountain of the same name.
ARDAMINE, a parish in co. Wexford, 4 m. SSE of Gorey. Area 4,215 acres. Pop. 1,705.
ARDANABIA, a river of France, an affluent of the Adour, into which it falls 9 m. above Bayonne, after a course of 15 m. It is navigable at high tide up to Portaberi.

ARDANUDJI, a town of Turkish Armenia, 40 m. N of Kars, and 90 m. NE of Erzerum, in N lat. 40° 44', E long. 43° 45'. It is a place of antiquity, and has a strong castle on a high rock in the steep valley of Narimani.
ARDARA, a village of Sardinia, in the district of Ireland, in co. Donegal, at the head of Loughrismore bay, 8 m. from Inver. Pop. 603.
ARDARGIE, a village of Perthshire, on the Ochils, 1½ m. S of Pitcaithly.

ARDATOF, a town of Asiatic Russia, on the river Alatyr, 128 m. W of Simbirsk, in N lat. 55° 20', E long. 42° 57'. Pop. 1,600.—Also a town of Russia ARD N lat. 54° 45′, E long. 45° 44′. Pop. 780.—Also a town in the gov. and 60 m. SSW of Nishni-Novgorod, on the Lemet river, in N lat. 50° 20', E long. 43° 4′. Pop. 1,000.
ARDBEAR, an inlet of the sea, on the coast of co. Galway, forming a fine natural harbour, at the head of which stands the thriving town of Clifden.

ARDBRACCAN, a small town and parish in the co. of Meath, 3 m. W of Navan, and 25 m. NW of Dublin. Area of p. 6,491 acres. Pop. 4,596. The bishop of Meath has a palace here; and in the burial ground the celebrated traveller, Pococke-who was bishop of Meath—lies interred.
ARDCANDRIES, a parish in co. Wexford, 31 m. WNW of Wexford. Area 1,227 acres. Pop. 286.
ARDCANNY, a parish in co. Limerick, 8 m. W by S of Limerick. Area 3,100 acres. Pop. 1,283.
ARDCARNE, a parish in co. Roscommon, 3\frac{1}{2} m. ESE of Boyle. Area 19,963 acres. Pop. 8,304.
ARDCATH, a parish and village in co. Meath, 7 m. SSW of Drogheda. Area 4,380 acres. Pop. 1,481.
ARDCAVAN, a parish in co. Wexford, 13 m. NE of Wexford. Area 2,458 acres. Pop. 947.

ARDCHATTAN, a parish in Argyleshire, stretching along the S shore of Loch Creran and both banks of Loch Etive. It is upwards of 30 m. in length, and from 15 to 20 m. in breadth. The greater part of it is mountainous and uncultivated, the land being principally in pasture. Ben Cruachan, one of the highest of the Scottish mountains, said to be 20 miles in circumference at its base, and to rise 3,390 ft. above the sea, is situated in the centre of this parish, which is distinguished for Druidical and other remains of antiquity. The supposed site of Beregonium is pointed out here; also the walls of a church in which St. Bede is said to have preached. Pop. in 1821, 1,663; in 1831, 2,420; in 1841, 1,461. Houses 300.
ARDCLACH, a parish in Nairnshire, 12 m. S by W of Forres. Pop., in 1821, 1,287; in 1841, 1,177.
ARDCLARE, a parish in co. Roscommon, 5 m. SSW of Tulsk. Area 8,544 acres. Pop. 2,555.
ARDCLINIS, a parish in co. Antrim, 6 m. N by W of Glenarm. Area 15,691 acres. Pop. 1,742. It is a mountainous district, having an average alt. of 500 or 600 ft. above sea-level.
ARDCOLLUM, or ARDCOLON, a parish in co. Wexford, 5 m. NNE of Wexford. Area 2,232 acres. Pop. 804.
ARDCRONY, a parish in co. Tipperary, 4 m. S of Borris-o'-Kane. Area 6,429 acres. Pop. 1,571.

ARDEBIL, or ARDABI'L, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Azerdbijan, on the r. bank of the Balutchai, 38 m. W of the Caspian, and 11 m. E of Tauris, in N lat. 38° 15′, E long. 48° 23′. Pop. in 1822, 3,000. It was at one time the residence of Persian kings, some of whom are entombed here. Sheltered by mountains from the hot winds which prevail on the shores of the Caspian, and its situation being in other respects advantageous, it has obtained the surname of Abadan-i-Firuz, or 'the Abode of happiness.' In consequence, however, of the great changes of temperature to which it is exposed, the town is not remarkably healthy; and has suffered much within the last few years from visitations of the plague. It is of some importance as an entrepôt in the caravantrade of Tiflis and Derbend with Ispahan and Teheand 19 m. W by N of Osieri. Pop. 264.—Also a v. ran. It contains a well-endowed college, a large caravansarai, and a bazar. It has a fine library, which was carried off to Russia after the surrender of the place to Count Soukhtaline, but was afterwards restored. The neighbouring country is fruitful, and well-cultivated, producing good crops of wheat, barley, and rice. The plain of A. is about 5,000 ft. above sea-level. ARDECLEAVE, a village in co. Londonderry, 5 | m. W by N of Coleraine. Pop. 450.

ARDECHE, a dep. in the south of France, on the west bank of the Rhone, between N lat. 44° 20' and 45° 20', and E long. 3° 50' and 4° 50'. It is bounded on the N by the dep. of Loire, from which it is separated by the Cevennes; on the E by Isère, Drome, and Vaucluse; on the S by Gard; and on the W by Lozere and Haute Loire. It is about 80 m. in length from NE to SW, and 40 m. in breadth. Area 538,988 hectares. Nearly the whole of this dep. lies in the valley of the Rhone, and is watered by that river, and its affluents the Ardeche, the Doux, and the Erieux; a small portion belongs to the basin of the Loire, and contains the sources of that river and of the Allier. It is a mountainous district stretching over the mountains of Vivarais, whose culminating points are the Mezen or Mezene, alt. 1,774 metres or 5,820 ft.; the Gerbier-de-Jones, alt. 1,562 metres or 5,124 ft.; and the plateau of Tanargue 5,013 ft. Its general slope is from the mountains on the W, to the Rhone on the E. In consequence of its very unequal elevation, this district exhibits a remarkable variety of climate. In the western parts, which are mountainous, the cold is extreme, and winter continues for nearly eight months. The vallies of the north are milder; and in the parts bordering on the Rhone, particularly toward the south, the heat is so great, that olives are grown in the open air,—a circumstance which takes place in no other part of France lying so far to the north. The cultivation of the silk-worm is an important branch of industry in this dep. The produce of silk in 1835 was 1,765,000 kilogrammes of cocoons, yielding 174,388 kil. of silk. The cultivation of the vine is also carried on in this part of the district with much success, and the white vines of St. Péray, and the red vines of Cornas, are much esteemed. Nearly 500,000 hectolitres are devoted to the cultivation of the vine. Some of the higher grounds are celebrated for their chestnutsthe marrons de Lyon—and other fruits; but the loftiest parts are either entirely barren, or afford very indifferent pasturage. In 1834, the cultivable soil was estimated at 129,000 hect.; meadows 44,000; vineyards 27,000; forests 98,000; under various cultivation 63,000; while the mountains and heaths amounted to 148,000. The geology of Ardèche is interesting from the numerous volcanic remains which are found in it. In the N and W granite, sandstone, iron-ore, and a very fine clay, occur. The volcanic rocks seem to have proceeded from the SE side of Mount Mezene in two principal embranchments, one to the S, and the other to the NNW. The latter covers a space 26 m. in length, with an average breadth of 6 m. It rests on granite either immediately or with the intervention of basalt. [Scrope.] This dep. contains also mines of lead, copper, antimony, iron, and manganese, some of which are wrought, together with coal, which is found in considerable quantities in the south-east. The manufacturing industry of the inhabitants is, however, the most important feature of this part of France. The tanneries and other manufactories for the dressing of skins are of considerable consequence. Paper-making has also been brought to great perfection here; the paper of Annonay is unsurpassed. Cotton-mills are in very extensive operation; and the art of dyeing the Turkey red, as here practised, is quite unrivalled. The production | Vendée, cant. of Les Herbiers. Pop. 1,542. and manufacture of silk is also carried on to a great extent; and the twist which is produced is nearly as much esteemed as that of Piedmont.-The roads, which are remarkably good, and the Rhone, afford an easy and advantageous outlet to the productions of its soil and manufactories. The pop., in 1836, was 353,752; in 1846, 364,416.—Ardeche was the an- $ ARD cient Vivarais, one of the divisions of Languedoc. It is divided into the three arrondissements of Privas, Largentière, and Tournon, and the electoral arrond. of Annonay, which are subdivided into 31 cantons, and 335 communes. It forms the dio. of the bishop of Viviers, suffragan of Avignon. Privas is the chief town.—The river Ardeche, which gives its name to the above district, rises in the Cevennes, and, after a course of about 60 m. E, S, and SE, falls into the Rhone, at St. Just, near Pont-Saint-Esprit. It is navigable for about 10 m. of the lower part of its course.

ARDEE, a barony, town, and parish, in co. Louth, 40 m. NW of Dublin. Area of barony 53,832 acres. Pop. 28,704. Houses 5,091. Area of p. 4,885 acres. Pop. 2,713.—The town stands on the river Dee, 11 m. NW of Drogheda, 68 m. SSW of Belfast. Pop. 3,679. Houses 707. An extensive corn-market is held here. It was anciently a walled town, and returned two members to the Irish parliament.

ARDE-KHU, a well-fortified town of Persia, in Farsistan, 150 m. E from Ispahan. It is situated in a fertile plain, and contains a caravansarai and a bazar, together with manufactories of a cloth for mattresses and tents. Pop. 5,000.

ARDELAN, a district of Persia, forming the NE division of Kurdistan. It is about 200 m. in length and 160 in breadth, stretching from the plain of Hamadan to the river Sharuk. The country-which surrounds the upper branches of the Suffid-rud-in general is hilly and uncultivated. A considerable portion of it consists of elevated table-lands which are covered with the flocks and tents of the Eeliauts from June till the end of August. Around Sennah, the cap., in N lat. 35° 12', however, there are numerous orchards, and some attention has there been paid to agriculture; tobacco is also cultivated to a small extent. The immense oak-forests with which the mountains W of Sennah are covered, produce abundance of timber; and the gall-nuts which are found in them are exported to India, and form a valuable article of commerce. The inhabitants are in a low state of civilization. They are addicted to war and rapine, and scarcely regard murder or particide as crimes. They are of a robust race: "and appear unchanged in their manners and customs by the twenty-three centuries which have elapsed since the days of Xenophon, who would have no difficulty, if permitted to return from the Elysian fields, to recognise the descendants of the enemies he encountered amidst these wilds." [Malcolm.] A tribe called Gheskee, in particular, are considered expert and daring robbers. The government, which is independent, is patriarchal in its form, though the wali or vali prince has the power of life and death, and is the most powerful vali or tributary of the Persian crown. He has above 50,000 sheep; and is "partner with every merchant and tradesman in his territory; or rather he is the general monopolizer." [Rich.] The territory is divided into seven boluks or provinces: viz., Inauroo, on the SW; Avroman, Merivan, Banna, and Sakez on the Tabriz road; Hassanabad, in which is the cap.; and Isfendabad, on the Hamadan road. Each of these prov. is subdivided into 4 or 5 mahalls or parishes. The court is held at Sennah.—Fraser. -Malcolm.-Kinneir.-Rich.
