ARDEN, the woodland district of Warwickshire, separated from the champaign district by the river Avon. In ancient times the whole country from the Avon to the Trent on the N, and to the Severn on the W, is supposed to have been covered with a forest. The name seems to have been a common Celtic designation for a forest or wooded region. head of Spencer's gulf. Admiralty island, in N lat. 58° 9', W long. 134° 51'.
ARDENBORG. See AARDENBURG.
ARDENELLE, a town of Hindostan, in the district of Coimbatur, 47 m. SE of Seringapatam.

ARDENNE, or Ardennes, a district of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, forming the elevated plateau in which the Semoy, the Lesse, the Ourte, and the Sure, have their sources. Its mean alt. is about 1,550 ft. above sea-level. Its highest elevation is La Baraque Michel, alt. 2,230 ft. The prevailing rocks are clay-slate, grauwacke, conglomerate, and quartz. Slaty rock is abundant. Iron ore and manganese are wrought in the district. A great portion of the surface is covered with heaths and marshes; and there are extensive forests of oak and beech. Cattle, sheep, and horses are reared in great numbers; but grain-crops are grown only to a limited extent. A large proportion of the inhabitants are employed in the manufacture of nails, fire-arms, and hardware goods. The Arduenna silva is mentioned by Cæsar and by Tacitus; and the name is frequently applied to the whole of the hilly region extending from Thiérache in Picardy, on the Meuse, to the banks of the Roer in Rhenish Prussia, including the mountain-district called the Eifel. The characteristic feature of this country is wildness; heathy and rocky hills, with dark rapid streams winding round them; vast forests abounding with deer, wild boars, and wolves; valleys of impoverished aspect at distant intervals; cottages thinly scattered among the valleys; and the ruins of feudal castles still frowning from rocky heights embosomed in wood.—Also a commune and village of Belgium, in Namur, on the Meuse. Pop. 3,400.

ARDENNES, a frontier-department of France, composed of a portion of the ancient Thiérache, of Upper Champagne, and of French Hainault; bounded on the N by Belgium; on the NE by the duchy of Luxemburg; on the SE by the dep. of the Meuse; on the S by Marne; and on the W by Aisne. Its extreme length from N to S is 69 m.; and its breadth from E to W nearly equal; presenting a total superficies of 517,385 hectares. It is intersected from SW to NW by a ramification of the Vosges chain, which divides the dep. into two nearly equal parts, and separates the basin of the Meuse, which stretches along the entire extent of its NE and N sections, from that of the Aisne, which extends in a curvilinear direction along the S of the former. The principal tributaries of the Meuse, belonging to this dep., are, on the r., the Chiers and the Semoy; and on the I., the Bar, the Vence, and the Sormonne. The most important affluents of the Aisne are the Aire and the Vaux on the r.; and on the l., the Retourne. Of these rivers the Meuse and Aisne only are navigable, and are united by the canal of Sedan, which extends to a considerable extent along the channel of the Bar. The Ton and the Serre, tributaries of the Oise, take their rise in the NW of the dep. The canal of A. connects the valleys of the Aisne and the Meuse. It begins at Donchery, on the latter river, and joins the Aisne at Semoy, whence it is continued on one side to Neufchatel, and on the other to Venziers. Its entire length is 61 m. It has 49 locks; and cost £561,280. -Coal, iron, slate, and stone are wrought in this dep.; the iron-works draw their fuel chiefly from the extensive forests. The cultivable land is estimated at 314,223 hectares; 95,461 hect. are under wood; 9,802 are disposed of in orchards; and 1,725 in vineyards. The total amount of grain raised in the dep. in 1837, was 3,140,000 hectolitres; but the ARD
ARDEN (Mount), a hill in S Australia, at the agricultural produce is not yet adequate to the local consumption. Cattle are extensively reared.

ARDEN (Point), a cape on the NW coast of The stock of oxen amounted to 300,000 head in 1837; horses, 57,000; sheep, 1,000,000. About 60,000 hectolitres of wine, 30,000 of cyder, and 177,000 of beer are annually manufactured. The chief articles of manufacture are woollen cloths, cashmeres, flannels, and merinoes, at Sedan and Rethel; cutlery and hardware at Charleville; porcelain, crystal, leather, candles, white lead and brandy are also manufactured to a considerable extent. Givet and Charleville are the chief entrepots.—The pop. in 1836 was 306,861; in 1846, 319,167.—The dep. is divided into the five arrond. of Rocroy, Mezieres, Sedan, Rethel, and Vouziers; and comprises 31 cant. and 478 com. With the arrond. of Rheims in Marne it forms the archbishopric of Rheims. In 1839, the amount of impôts directs raised in this dep. was 1,720,758 francs. It is in the jurisdiction of the assize court of Metz.
ARDENNO, a town in the Valteline, 4 m. N of Morbegno, in the valley of the Maseno. Pop. 600.
ARDENSAN, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the prov. of Aladulia, 38 m. NW of Arzingan.
ARDENTES-SAINT-MARTIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre, cant. of Ardentes- Saint-Vincent. Pop. 1,054.

ARDENTES-SAINT-VINCENT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Indre, arrond. of Chateauroux. Pop. of cant., comprising 10 com., 7,599 in 1831; of com. and town in 1846, 2,162. The town is on the Indre, 9 m. SE of Chateauroux.
ARDERO, a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra, 7 m. S of Gierace.
ARDERSIER, a parish in Inverness-shire, 12 m. E of Fort-George. Pop. in 1821, 1,387; in 1841, 1,475, of whom 716 were in the village of Campbelltown, and 256 in garrison in Fort George.

ARDES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, arrond. of Issoire. Pop. of cant., comprising 16 com., in 1831, 11,172; of com. in 1846, 1,793. The town is 11 m. SW of Issoire. It lies in a fertile country, and has a very considerable inland trade. The surrounding scenery is beautiful, and in the neighbourhood are numerous volcanic productions.—Also a town in Albania, on a river of the same name, rising near Mount Khomi, and joining the Vojutza, in N lat. 40° 22'.—Also a barony on the coast of co. Down, comprising the peninsula between Lough Strangford and the Irish sea. Length 19 m.; greatest breadth 5 m. Area 68,159 acres. Pop. in 1841, 46,875.
ARDESH. See ARDISHER.
ARDETZ, or ARDEZ, a town in the Grisons, on the l. bank of the Inn, 36 m. E of Chur, celebrated for its honey. Pop. 560.
ARDEVON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, on the bay of Mont-Saint-Michel. Pop. 480.

ARDFERT, a town and parish in co. Kerry, 4 m. NNW of Tralee. Area of p. 10,134 acres. Pop. 7,493. This town was formerly a bishop's see, and the capital of the county. At present it does not rank above a village in extent and pop., and has no corporate officer. Pop. in 1841, 655. The ruins of several religious edifices exist here. The bishopric of A. is consolidated with that of Aghadoe.
ARDFIELD, a parish in co. Cork, 5 m. S by E of Clonakilty. Area 2,645 acres. Pop. 2,460. It runs out to the extremity of the peninsula on the E side of Ross bay.
ARDFINNAN, a parish in co. Tipperary, on the Suir, 61 m. WSW of Clonmel. Area 1813 acres. Pop. 1,214; of whom 530 were in the village of A.

ARDGLASS, a seaport town and parish in co. Down. Area of p. 1,137 acres. Pop. 1,433.—The town of A., which is 7 m. SE by S of Downpatrick, was formerly a place of great strength and importance, as the remains of its huge walls and fortifications seem to denote. Its harbour is on a small rocky creek, about 150 fath. wide, and extending at high water 500 fath. inland. It is the rendezvous of the Down fisheries. Steam-passage from A. to Liverpool may be made in 9 hours.
ARDGUIN, a parish in co. Down, lying along Lough Strangford. Area 3,043 acres. Pop. 916.
ARDIA, a town in the Campagna di Roma, 5 m. S of Albana, 18 m. N from Rome.
ARDIALLE, a hamlet of France, in the com. of Puylaurens, in the dep. of Tarn. Pop. 1,000.
ARDIERE, a river of France, in the dep. of Ain, which runs into the Saone near Belleville, after a course of 20 m. from W to E.
ARDILA, a river rising in Spanish Estremadura, in the prov. of Badajoz, and joining the Guadiana on the l. near Mouza in Portugal, after a course of 75 m.
ARDILLATS (LES), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Rhône, 17 m. NW of Villefranche. Pop. 1,112.
ARDIN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, cant. of Coulonges. Pop. 1,870. There are marble-quarries here.
ARDINGY, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 52 m. SSW of Tanjore, in N lat. 10° 9'.
ARDINGLEY, a parish in Sussex, 4 m. NE from Cuckfield. Area 4,780 acres. Pop. 742.
ARDINGTON, a parish in Berkshire, 3 m. E of Wantage. Area 1,310 acres. Pop. 405.
ARDIS, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Diarbekir, on a branch of the Euphrates, 35 m. NW of Diarbekir.

ARDISH, or Arjish, a town of Armenia, on the N shore of Lake Van. The casaba or citadel is inhabited by about 100 Mahommedan and a few Armenian families. The houses within the walls are half under ground. The plain of A. is rapidly gaining on the lake.

ARDISHER, a village of Armenia, 15 m. S by E of Erivan, inhabited by a colony of Persians from the district of Salmás. It is on a small stream which enters the Aras a little above Khorviráb, and which the missionaries Dwight and Smith identify with the Medzamor. Within a few rods of A. are the ruins of an ancient city, with double walls, which enclose an extensive area, marking the site probably of the ancient Tovin, the cap. of the country from A. D. 350 to 859, which was situated to the N of Ardashad, or Artaxata, a city at the junction of the Medzamor and Aras,—Missionary Researches in Armenia.
ARDIVORAN HEAD, the northern point of the Scottish island of North Uist, in N lat. 57° 41', W long. 7° 20'.

ARDJA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, on the l. bank of the Euphrates, in the pashalik of Bassora, 45 m. NW of Bassora. The stream of the Euphrates is at this point nearly exhausted by the numerous canals which are led off from it for the purpose of irrigation.

ARDJICH, a river of Upper Wallachia, which, rising in the frontiers of Transylvania, falls into the Danube, about 2 m. below Toutowrkai, after a course of about 180 m.—Also a town on the above river, 63 m. SW from Cronstadt. It is protected by a strong castle, and was formerly the residence of the native princes, but is much decayed.

ARDJISH-DAGH, a mountain in Karamania, the highest in Asia Minor. Its summit-which appears to be about 10 m. from its average base—bears S 7° W from Kaisariyeh in N lat. 38° 41′ 40". Mr. Hamilton estimates its alt. at 12,809 ft. Mr. Ainsworth estimates its area at 300 sq. m., and its circumf. at | and formidable kingdom, till subdued, in the begin- ARD 60 m. It is principally of volcanic origin. Its summit is covered with perpetual snow; and in winter the snow-line descends to within a few hundred feet of the plain, which has an elev. of 4,200 ft., according to Hamilton. It is the ancient Argœus.
ARDKEEN, or ARDQUIN, a parish in co. Down, on Lough Strangford. Area 4,801 acres. Pop. 2,238.
ARDKILL, a parish in co. Kildare. Area 5,848 acres. Pop. 1,214.
ARDLE, a river in Perthshire, which, after watering Strathardle, unites with the Shee to form the Ericht.
ARDLEIGH, a parish in Essex, 5 m. NE from Colchester. Area 5,100 acres. Pop. 1,605.
ARDLEY, a parish in Oxfordshire, 4½ m. NW of Bicester. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 168.
ARDMAYLE, a parish in co. Tipperary, 3 m. N by W of Cashel. Area 4,941 acres. Pop. 1,757.
ARDMEANACH, or Black-Isle, -so called from its bleak, moorland character,—a district of Rossshire and Cromarty, comprehending 8 parishes, and forming a barren peninsula on the sea-coast.

ARDMORE, a parish and village in co. Waterford. Area of p. 24,215 acres. Pop. 8,737. The village is situated at the W end of Ardmore bay, 4 m. ENE of Youghal. Pop. 716. It contains several ecclesiastical antiquities, and a round tower.
ARDMORE HEAD, a noted promontory in the above parish, forming the E side of Youghal harbour, in N lat. 51° 37', W long. 7° 43'.—Also a cape on the W coast of Skye, in N lat. 57° 37'.
ARDMULCAN, a parish in co. Meath, 3 m. NE of Navan. Area 3,583 acres. Pop. 1,038.
ARDNAGEEHY, a parish in co. Cork, 6 m. SW of Rathcormac. Area 16,335 acres. Pop. 4,798.
